does: This paper develops SWE-RM, a reward model that provides execution-free feedback for software engineering agents without requiring unit tests. The authors create reward models trained to score code trajectories and solutions, then evaluate them across both test-time scaling (where the model selects among candidate solutions) and reinforcement learning (where the model guides policy optimization). They identify that performance under these two settings diverges significantly: verifiers optimized for test-time scaling selection ability fail to provide effective RL training signals. The paper proposes additional design principles beyond trajectory ranking to make reward models effective for RL training, enabling agents to improve through learned reward signals rather than sparse test-based feedback.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using execution-based feedback and test-time scaling to improve coding agents, where models like AlphaCode and recent code generation systems leverage unit tests as reward signals. The paper directly responds to limitations of this paradigm—sparse feedback, dependence on comprehensive test suites, and inability to distinguish between unsuccessful attempts. It connects to research on reward modeling and its application to RL in language model domains, acknowledging that prior work has explored execution-free rewards for code but primarily in simpler settings. The gap the authors address is the mismatch between what makes good verifiers for ranking versus what drives effective policy learning, a distinction rooted in RL theory but previously unexamined for software engineering agents.
enables: This work enables more efficient and scalable training of coding agents by removing the requirement for extensive unit test case collection and execution infrastructure. By making reward signals denser and more informative than test-based feedback, the approach could accelerate development of agents that handle real-world software engineering tasks where comprehensive test suites are unavailable. The identification of properties crucial for RL-effective reward models opens directions for better verifier design and could extend to other domains where execution-based feedback is expensive. The execution-free paradigm potentially enables reward models to provide richer gradient signals for policy optimization, setting up future work on combining test-time scaling with more sophisticated RL algorithms and exploring what architectural and training choices best satisfy both selection and learning objectives.
